title: Lasianthaea salvinii
taxonomy: Lasianthaea salvinii is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. This plant belongs to the genus Lasianthaea, which is a part of the larger clade of daisies and sunflowers. Further, Lasianthaea salvinii is a member of the subfamily Asteroideae and tribe Heliantheae. Within the family Asteraceae, Lasianthaea salvinii is considered to be a unique species that is native to Mexico. By classifying Lasianthaea salvinii in this way, we can understand its evolutionary relationships and place within the plant kingdom.

intro: Lasianthaea salvinii, also known as Salvin's daisy or Mexican daisy, is a plant species within the Asteraceae family, native to Mexico. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to its use in treating various ailments, such as wounds, skin infections, and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Lasianthaea salvinii include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which have been shown to possess antioxidant and cytotoxic activities. Traditionally, this plant is prepared as a tea or infusion, and it may also be used in tinctures or capsules, making it a popular ingredient in various herbal supplements and remedies.
